title: 講下 G6PD 缺乏症 (蠶豆症)

replies.msg:
搵唔撚到蠶豆


乜野叫做G6PD？
G6PD中文全名叫做 葡萄糖-6-磷酸脱氫酶 ，雖然話就係一隻酵素，但係佢o係一個化學反應鏈度扮演緊一個好重要嘅角色，而呢條鏈就係用黎分解多餘嘅活性氧化物，如果冇咗呢隻野，就咁大劑。



都唔知你，又會講到活性氧化物。

點解要考慮活性氧化物？好簡單咁講，太多活性氧化物會傷害到好多細胞，最明顯嘅傷害就係溶血性貧血 → 唔夠紅血球 → 好X多問題。當然，紅血球只係其中一個目標，另外果啲會再討論。

咁好喇，都係唔明乜野係活性氧化物。如果你睇一睇上圖串英文，你會見到左上角有H2O2，即係雙氧水，呢樣野唔分解就有機會變成游離基。睇得廣告多都知道游離基係會傷害到細胞，都唔明白幾廿年有班友好意思去提飲雙氧水醫病。

咁好啦，活性氧化物係邊度黎架？

下面張表有一堆關活活性氧化物來源嘅例子。



好老實，如果你中學冇讀過Ｂｉｏ，我真係唔想逐個翻譯左手邊果PART。

咁右手邊果行講緊乜？除左體內會生產活性氧化物，體外好似
—　食煙，紫外光， 游離輻射 ，污染物，臭氧層，化療，有機溶劑，酒精同金屬都係個源頭。不過影響幾大就唔知。

入正題未？

ＯＫ，廢話講完，係時候抄下市面已經有嘅資料再炒埋一碟過你食。

資料１衛生署
http://www.fhs.gov.hk/tc_chi/mulit_med/000018.html

“ ...衞生署醫學遺傳服務遺傳篩選組，會為在醫院管理局醫院出生的寶寶，抽取臍帶血化驗，就可以知道寶寶是否患有這一類的遺傳病...”

“... 急性溶血會產生過量膽紅素，而過高的膽紅素，可進入初生嬰兒的腦部，會造成一些永久性的傷害，例如失聰、四肢活動不良，智力受損，如能及早發現和接受治療 ...”

“... 溶血症主要徵狀，包括眼白泛黃、小便變茶色，除此以外，寶寶可能看起來神情呆滯和經常昏睡，而較大的小朋友可能會面色蒼白、過度疲倦、呼吸不暢順和心跳加速等，如留意到有以上的徵狀，應立即求醫。”

“ 在正常情況下，可以繼續以用母乳餵哺寶寶，如果媽媽需要服用綠卡內的中西藥，便需要暫停餵哺母乳，以免藥物經母乳傳給寶寶，不過媽媽要繼續擠奶以保持足夠的奶量，讓停藥後可以繼續餵哺寶寶。”

“ 至於日常用品方面，除了綠卡提到物品外，現時沒有證據顯示香熏、衣物柔順劑、含薄荷的食用品、含甘菊的紙巾和尿片、防蟲劑、蚊香、家用藥油等，都會使G6PD缺乏症患者產生溶血情況，為安全計，使用前都要查清楚成分，如有疑問最好都是向生產商或醫生查詢。”

“ 如果G6PD缺乏症患者食了某些引致溶血食物後，沒有出現問題，並不代表他們可繼續放心吃那種食物，是否會產生溶血症，除了患者身體狀況外，亦會受到食物分量和其他因素所影響，所以為了安全起見，還是不要吃可引致溶血食物。”


“ G6PD缺乏症是一種性連鎖隱性遺傳病，性連鎖遺傳病，即是...

自己睇圖



即係得個老竇中招，個阿媽冇事 → 生女就有呢條基因，但係冇事；生仔就完全冇事

　得個阿媽有果條基因，個老竇冇事 → 1/2 機會個仔會有呢隻病，1/2 機會個女會帶呢條基因。

咁係咪即係女人唔會有呢隻病？ＮＯ，前題係老竇阿媽都有呢隻病…”

“ G6PD缺乏症是一種遺傳病，而不是免疫系統出現問題 ... 所以你們要記住隨身攜帶綠卡 ...”

“ ...例如蠶豆酥；第四，家居用品，就要避免讓寶寶接觸到臭丸，或含有萘類的防蟲片...”

https://www.aafp.org/afp/2005/1001/p1277.html#afp20051001p1277-t3

“Treatment

The main treatment for G6PD deficiency is avoidance of oxidative stressors. Rarely, anemia
may be severe enough to warrant a blood transfusion.

仲有更多treatment, 想知更多直接去搵醫生問！”

咁蠶豆症有D乜野藥/食物唔食得？

自己睇綠卡，同埋Table 3,6。基本上我個立場係極反對食中藥，遲下會講點解。



點解個ＢＢ有黃疸病要照燈

頭先講左活性氧物會消滅紅血球，咁之後會點？



條路會好慢長，最後變成bilirubin，再畀肝加工過之後痾屎出去（當然有部份會吸收返）。但係ＢＢ個肝未夠成熟，冇得加工 → bilirubin累積係體內 → 黃疸病。

咁點搞啊？照燈係咪真係掂架？

多得bilirubin 個化學結構，只要照左燈，個bilirubin就會燈到有同分異構體 / bilirubin 會被氧化。氧化左就可以算痾尿排出，或者照得耐會再進一步氧化成為 Z-lumirubin，一樣可以算痾尿。

More than 200 mutations that cause glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency have been identified in the G6PD gene. Almost all of these mutations lead to changes in single building blocks (amino acids) in the glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase enzyme. These changes disrupt the normal structure and function of the enzyme or reduce the amount of the enzyme produced in cells.

Without enough functional glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase, red blood cells are unable to protect themselves from the damaging effects of reactive oxygen species. The damaged cells are likely to rupture and break down prematurely (undergo hemolysis). Factors such as infections, certain drugs, and ingesting fava beans can increase the levels of reactive oxygen species, causing red blood cells to undergo hemolysis faster than the body can replace them. This loss of red blood cells causes the signs and symptoms of hemolytic anemia, which is a characteristic feature of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.

A: Red-cell destruction in favism is a complex process, but it has gradually been clarified. replies.msg:
The G6PD / NADPH pathway is the only source of reduced glutathione in red blood cells (erythrocytes). The role of red cells as oxygen carriers puts them at substantial risk of damage from oxidizing free radicals except for the protective effect of G6PD/NADPH/glutathione.
When all remaining reduced glutathione is consumed, enzymes and other proteins (including hemoglobin) are subsequently damaged by the oxidants, leading to cross-bonding and protein deposition in the red cell membranes. Damaged red cells are phagocytosed and sequestered (taken out of circulation) in the spleen. The hemoglobin is metabolized to bilirubin (causing jaundice at high concentrations). The red cells rarely disintegrate in the circulation, so hemoglobin is rarely excreted directly by the kidney, but this can occur in severe cases, causing acute renal failure.

What are the symptoms of hemolytic anemia?

G6PD can cause hemolytic anemia. This is when the red blood cells break down faster than they are made. Symptoms of hemolytic anemia include:

Pale skin
Yellowing of the skin, eyes, and mouth (jaundice)
Dark-colored urine
Fever
Weakness
Dizziness
Confusion
Trouble with physical activity
Enlarged spleen and liver
Increased heart rate
Heart murmur
The symptoms of hemolytic anemia may look like other health problems. Always see your healthcare provider for a diagnosis.

I Have G6PD Deficiency, Can I Still Donate Blood?


People with Glucose-6-Phosphate Dehydrogenase Deficiency (G6PD) can make blood donations. However, we would usually advise these donors to donate plasma or platelets instead of whole blood.

This is because G6PD affects the body's red blood cells, making them more vulnerable to bio-chemical stress. The red blood cells are less robust as a result, and are susceptible to damage and destruction when exposed to certain medication or acute infections. As such the red cells of people with G6PD are not suitable for transfusion.

G6PD is a mild genetic blood disorder and is an inherited trait.

A: Red-cell destruction in favism is a complex process, but it has gradually been clarified. The fava bean plant contains high concentrations of two β-glucosides (up to 2% in dry weight): vicine and convicine. On ingestion of fava beans, vicine and convicine undergo hydrolysis by glucosidases present both in the beans and in the gastrointestinal tract, releasing the respective aglycones: divicine and isouramil. These ... compounds are also capable of triggering a favism attack."

What are the symptoms of hemolytic anemia?

G6PD can cause hemolytic anemia. This is when the red blood cells break down faster than they are made. Symptoms of hemolytic anemia include:

Pale skin
Yellowing of the skin, eyes, and mouth (jaundice)
Dark-colored urine
Fever
Weakness
Dizziness
Confusion
Trouble with physical activity
Enlarged spleen and liver
Increased heart rate
Heart murmur
The symptoms of hemolytic anemia may look like other health problems. Always see your healthcare provider for a diagnosis.
